title: How To Graph Systems Of Equations - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of graphing linear systems, finding intersections, parallel lines, coincident lines, and matplotlib graphing

questions.question: When graphing two linear equations, the point where the lines cross represents:

options.text: The solution to the system of equations
The y-intercept of the first equation

questions.explanation: The intersection point of two lines represents the solution to the system — the (x, y) values that satisfy both equations simultaneously.

questions.question: Two lines have slopes m₁ = 3 and m₂ = 3 but different y-intercepts. How many solutions does this system have?
questions.explanation: Lines with the same slope but different y-intercepts are parallel — they never intersect. A system with parallel lines has no solution and is called inconsistent.

questions.question: Which Python library function is used to display a plot in matplotlib?
questions.explanation: In matplotlib, `plt.show()` displays the figure with all plotted elements. It is typically called after setting up the plot with plt.plot(), labels, and other configurations.

questions.question: If two equations represent the same line (coincident lines), the system has:
questions.explanation: Coincident lines are the same line, so every point on the line is a solution. This gives infinitely many solutions. The system is called dependent.

questions.question: To graph y = 2x + 1 using matplotlib, which code is correct?
questions.explanation: The correct syntax is `plt.plot(x, y)` where x and y are arrays of values. For y = 2x + 1, you would first create an array of x values (e.g., using numpy.linspace), compute y = 2*x + 1, and then call `plt.plot(x, y)`.

questions.question: What is the graphical solution to the system y = x + 1 and y = -x + 5?
questions.explanation: Set the equations equal: x + 1 = -x + 5, so 2x = 4, giving x = 2. Substituting back: y = 2 + 1 = 3. The intersection point is (2, 3).

questions.question: In matplotlib, what does `plt.xlabel('x-axis')` do?

options.text: Sets the range of the x-axis
Adds a label to the x-axis

questions.explanation: `plt.xlabel('x-axis')` adds a descriptive label to the x-axis of the plot. Similarly, `plt.ylabel()` labels the y-axis and `plt.title()` adds a title.

questions.question: Two lines with slopes m₁ = 2 and m₂ = -1/2 are:
questions.explanation: Two lines are perpendicular when the product of their slopes is -1. Here, 2 × (-1/2) = -1, so the lines are perpendicular (they meet at a 90° angle).

questions.question: Which numpy function creates an array of evenly spaced values, commonly used for generating x-values for graphing?
questions.explanation: `numpy.linspace(start, stop, num)` creates an array of `num` evenly spaced values from `start` to `stop`. For example, `np.linspace(-10, 10, 100)` creates 100 points from -10 to 10, ideal for plotting smooth curves.

questions.question: When graphing a system of equations, if the lines have different slopes, the system is:
questions.explanation: Two lines with different slopes will always intersect at exactly one point. This system is called independent and consistent — it has a unique solution at the intersection point.
